title: What an American thinks about Guatemala

transcript.text: Okay, hello, this video, what an American thinks about Guatemala.
I think Guatemala is my favorite country in Central America.
I was in Guatemala like three and a half, like four months or something like that.
I was there three months and then I left and went to Honduras because I had to every three months
And then went back for like another month.
So, Guatemala is my favorite place in Central America because it's the most native, most Indian.
They maintain their culture the most.
They still have their traditional dress.
And I knew that before I even went down there.
Oh, no.
All right, now I'm on my own.
I went down there, but before I went down there, I knew that it was like Guatemalan clothing, we called it,
Because they had like a shop in Boulder.
And it's really pretty.
It's really loose-fitting, colorful pants and shirt.
And like matching shirt and pants.
I had a little suit and I bought one when I was there too.
But, yeah, a lot of native cultures have that.
But they have their own little pattern, which signifies the little valley they're from.
All over the world it's like that.
But they have maintained that tradition.
And, yeah, you could see them dressed in their little bags and their clothing.
It was pretty.
And it's really hilly.
I stayed in Lake Atitlan for three months.
And that's a beautiful place.
Big lake surrounded by really steep hills and villages around the edge of it.
I was in San Pedro.
And I climbed a mountain one day and I looked over and there was plains.
It was flat and below the lake for the rest of the way.
But all of the part that I saw was really, really hilly.
And there's lots of native communities there.
But they were all living up above the lake, which I thought was interesting.
All of the lakeside, I guess the prime territory was like in San Marcos and San Pedro was inhabited by tourist expats.
And then the Indians had their own little village up higher, I guess because they were poorer.
I went over to San Marcos and I was walking around the Indian village above them and that was really basic.
And they seemed really excited to see me.
Like they weren't used to seeing people walking through.
I think it was walking through.
But I don't recall any of them speaking English.
I just speak Spanish with all of them.
And, yeah, it's really, really, really cheap.
And Guatemala City wasn't very pretty.
I didn't spend a whole lot of time there, but it looked really big.
I think it's pretty dangerous, too, that place.
When people climb mountains, they usually get mugged.
Or they get mugged a lot of the times by guys with guns.
And when people climb the mountain outside of Granada,
They have to bring escorts with machine guns with them.
They did have a war there, like in the 80s.
And I think there's some political problems over there.
And maybe they're not so happy with tourists having more money than they do.
But they seemed friendly enough.
I didn't have any bad experiences with any natives.
There sure are a lot of expats there, though.
It's weird.
It's a strange thing to witness, all the expats along the lake
And all the Indians higher up.
And it's not really, you know, the expat bars where all you see are expats.
And then if you go up to the native bar, it's...
Actually, I didn't see any of those.
There are lots of churches there, though, in San Pedro.
I think I counted, like, 13 churches, little bitty churches.
So they're really religious over there.
And each town along the lake was different.
It had a totally different feel.
Like, Santiago is where all of the kind of more native culture was.
The witches.
San Marcos was where all these, like, new-ager expats were.
And then the native village was really small over there, actually.
But they had prices for tourists, different prices for tourists for stuff
That I think they were just starting to implement
Because it was getting more popular with the tourists.
I don't know what I think about that.
We climbed a mountain.
It was the tallest mountain in Central America.
And we were riding the bus back, and that bus was going way too fast.
Some crazy driving.
We were actually skidding around the corners.
I could hear the tire skidding around the corners on the edge of a cliff.
And it scared the hell out of me.
I was terrified.
And while I was there in San Pedro, two buses did collide into each other,
Driving around that lake.
And, like, 50 people died.
I think everybody in both of the buses died.
And then they had a big funeral procession that day.
One day while I was there, I was on acid.
And right when I started tripping, I could hear the people wailing,
Oh, no, in the funeral procession.
And I saw the bodies go by.
So, yeah, they could, they should slow their buses down.
But Granada was a pretty place.
Really touristy.
A lot of tourists.
A lot of, like, Israelis.
It's a really old, old town.
Anyways, I was there such a long time ago.
That's all I remember.
But the expat scene in Lake Adderland is interesting.
Because it's different expats for each town.
Like the druggies in San Pedro and the new agers in San Marcos.
And, like, the short timers over there in Gringolandia or whatever.
Gringoteca.
Okay.
So that's what I think about Guatemala.
Guatemala.
Guatemala.
